narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your Year 1 school decile of 3 (below the average of about 5.7), Year 3 decile of 2 (below the typical 5.5), undergraduate GPA of 3.0 (slightly under the 3.21 average), LSAT score of 29 (well below the 36.7 average), lower-middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 52.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This probability tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 academic decile ranking of 2, which stood out as a strong signal of underperformance relative to peers, signaling potential challenges in handling rigorous legal coursework. Next in importance was your family income in the lower-middle bracket, which actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, perhaps reflecting resilience or motivation from your circumstances. Your Year 1 decile of 3 also contributed toward the failure outlook, reinforcing concerns about early academic momentum compared to the average student's stronger start. Overall, these grade-related deciles carried the most weight because they directly correlate with bar exam readiness in the model's view, outweighing positives like your full-time commitment, which is common among 93% of students. While your LSAT and GPA raised flags against the averages, it was the consistent below-average decile performance that dominated the decision. This placed you below typical admitted students, who often enter with higher deciles and scores indicating a smoother path to bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the 37 range, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted coursework or tutoring, and highlighting any personal achievements that demonstrate growth despite your background. Reapplying after addressing these core academic areas could significantly shift your predicted odds, opening doors at schools that value determined applicants like you.
